introduction: Difficulty in falling asleep or staying asleep. May be primary and unrelated to any physical or mental disorder. May relate to a mental disorder, medical or physical conditions. May be an adverse effect of medication (or psychoactive substances). A common, often chronic problem; tends to increase with age.

features: Early insomnia: difficulty in initiating sleep
Middle insomnia: difficulty in going back to sleep after waking up at night
Terminal insomnia: early awakening, commonly 2 hours or more before desiring to do so

investigations: Mainly of the presumed underlying cause(s)

goals: To improve sleep, especially quality (sleep satisfaction) and quantity (duration of sleep)
Avoid sedatives: use for only short periods when indicated
non_drug: Avoid habits and lifestyles that promote insomnia
drug: Longer-acting benzodiazepines e.g. Diazepam at low doses: 2.5 - 10 mg for no more than 2 - 3 weeks for middle insomnia
supportive_measures: Relaxation therapy: a useful adjunct for the most common forms of insomnia

differential_diagnoses: Useful to consider possible aetiological factors: medical, mental, situational, environmental
complications: Decline in overall wellbeing and quality of life
prevention: Caution with alcohol and psychoactive substances, such as coffee, kolanut
Discourage misuse of "sleeping pills" e.g., bromazepam, diazepam
